questions.specification: A global banking corporation is planning to replace its legacy customer database with a modern, cloud-integrated system.

parts.content: Outline **one** benefit for the corporation of adopting a Software as a Service (SaaS) model for the new database system.
parts.markscheme:
- Lower initial costs/capital expenditure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- because the bank does not need to invest in its own server hardware or data centers <marks>1 mark</marks>

**OR**

- Ease of maintenance and updates <marks>1 mark</marks>
- as the service provider handles all software patches, security updates, and version upgrades automatically <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award **[2 max]**.

parts.content: State **one** reason why high-quality user documentation is necessary for the new system.
parts.markscheme:
- To reduce the frequency of support requests/calls to the help desk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- To improve employee productivity by allowing them to troubleshoot common issues independently <marks>1 mark</marks>
- To provide a reference for training new employees who join after the initial rollout <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award **[1 max]**.

parts.content: Outline **one** disadvantage of using self-instruction (e.g., using manuals or pre-recorded tutorials) as the primary method of user training.
parts.markscheme:
- **Lack of immediate feedback** <marks>1 mark</marks>
- users cannot ask questions or get clarification if they misunderstand a complex part of the system <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Requires high levels of user motivation** <marks>1 mark</marks>
- employees may procrastinate or fail to complete the training if they are not supervised or scheduled into a session <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award **[2 max]**.

parts.content: Describe **two** types of feasibility study that should be conducted during the planning phase of the database project.
parts.markscheme:
- **Economic feasibility** <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Evaluates the financial aspects, such as whether the projected benefits and cost savings outweigh the development and maintenance costs <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Technical feasibility** <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Assesses whether the bank has the necessary hardware, software, and technical expertise to support the new cloud-integrated system <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Operational feasibility** <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Examines how well the proposed system fits the current business environment and whether staff will be able to integrate it into their daily workflows <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Legal/Regulatory feasibility** <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Checks if the system complies with data protection laws (e.g., GDPR) and banking regulations regarding data storage <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award **[4 max]**.
Mark as 2 and 2 (1 mark for identifying the type and 1 mark for the description).

parts.content: Evaluate **two** types of testing (e.g., Alpha, Beta, Dry run, Unit testing) that should be performed before the system is released to the entire corporation. Your answer should include the advantages and disadvantages of each.
parts.markscheme:
**Alpha testing** <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Advantage**: Performed internally by developers or a dedicated QA team, allowing major bugs to be fixed before any customer or real-world data is exposed to the system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Disadvantage**: It may not identify issues that arise from real-world usage patterns or varied hardware configurations that exist outside the lab <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Beta testing** <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Advantage**: Involves real users testing the system in their actual working environment, providing feedback on usability and finding bugs that internal testers missed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Disadvantage**: There is a risk of data loss or security leaks if the beta version is unstable, and negative user experiences can damage morale before the system even launches <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Dry run testing** <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Advantage**: Allows the logic of the system's processes to be tested on paper or mentally by the developer to ensure the algorithm works as intended without using computer resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Disadvantage**: It is time-consuming for complex systems and cannot detect technical errors related to hardware or environment integration <marks>1 mark</marks>

**User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Advantage**: Ensures the system actually meets the business requirements and specific needs of the end-users before final sign-off <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Disadvantage**: Can be difficult to manage if different departments have conflicting views on what the final system should do <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award **[6 max]**.
Mark as 3 and 3 (1 mark for type, 1 mark for advantage, and 1 mark for disadvantage).

<note>
Accept other suitable types of testing and relevant evaluations.
</note>

questions.specification: IB Computer Science - Topic 1: System fundamentals

parts.content:
A multi-national corporation is upgrading its legacy human resources (HR) software. The system consists of three distinct modules: Payroll, Recruitment, and Employee Performance. The company has decided to implement the new system using a **phased conversion**.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of using a phased conversion for this upgrade.
parts.markscheme:
**Advantages:**

- **Reduced risk of total system failure**: If a critical error occurs during the implementation of one module (e.g., Payroll), the other departments (Recruitment and Performance) are unaffected and can continue working on the old system. <marks>1</marks>
- **Incremental training**: Staff members only need to learn the functionality of the specific module being introduced, making the transition less overwhelming and reducing training downtime. <marks>1</marks>
- **Refinement of process**: Lessons learned from the implementation of the first module can be used to improve the rollout of subsequent modules. <marks>1</marks>

**Disadvantages:**

- **Compatibility and integration issues**: Temporary software bridges or interfaces must often be created to allow the new modules to communicate with the remaining legacy modules, which increases technical complexity. <marks>1</marks>
- **Extended implementation timeline**: The total time required to fully implement the system across the entire organization is much longer than a direct changeover. <marks>1</marks>
- **Data inconsistency**: During the transition period, data may be split between two different systems, which can lead to confusion or errors in reporting if not managed carefully. <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [4 max]. Award 2 marks for advantages and 2 marks for disadvantages.</note>

<examTip>When discussing implementation methods for a modular system, consider how the modules interact with each other during the transition phase.</examTip>

questions.specification: IB Computer Science Topic 1.1.5: Describe the various methods of implementation.

parts.content: A small medical clinic is replacing its patient management software. Identify **two** disadvantages of using the **direct changeover** method for this transition.
parts.markscheme:
- There is no backup system available if the new software fails, which could lead to a loss of critical patient data or a total halt in clinic operations. <marks>1</marks>
- Staff may struggle to adapt to the new interface immediately, and without the old system as a reference, errors are more likely to occur during the learning curve. <marks>1</marks>
- There is no opportunity to verify the accuracy and reliability of the new system's outputs by comparing them against the old system. <marks>1</marks>
- If the new system is found to be completely inadequate after launch, reverting to the previous system is often difficult or impossible due to data changes. <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Maximum 2 marks</note>

questions.specification: A nationwide retail chain is planning to transition from its traditional local cash register system to a centralized cloud-based inventory and sales management platform.

parts.content: Outline why **technical feasibility** must be considered before the project begins.
parts.markscheme:
- To determine if the organization's existing hardware or network infrastructure can support the requirements of the new cloud-based system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- To identify whether the technical skills required to implement and maintain the system are currently available within the organization <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of **questionnaires** to gather requirements from the large number of employees who will use the new platform.
parts.markscheme:
- Questionnaires allow for the efficient collection of data from a large geographic group of employees simultaneously <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The responses provide quantifiable data that can be easily analyzed to identify common needs or concerns <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, they lack the flexibility of interviews, meaning researchers cannot ask follow-up questions to clarify ambiguous or unexpected answers <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline **one** disadvantage of using **external training** providers to teach staff how to use the new system.
parts.markscheme:
- External training can be very expensive due to high consultancy fees and the cost of travel for staff <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The training materials may be generic and not tailored to the specific business workflows or unique features of the company's internal setup <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
An international bank continues to use a mainframe computer from the 1980s for its core transactions.

Outline **two** problems that the bank may face by continuing to use this legacy system.
parts.markscheme:
<note>
Award **[1]** for each valid problem outlined, up to **[2 max]**.
</note>

- **Maintenance difficulties**:
It may be difficult or expensive to find programmers familiar with outdated programming languages (e.g., COBOL) needed to update the system. <marks>1</marks>

- **Incompatibility issues**:
The legacy system may not be able to integrate or share data seamlessly with modern web-based or mobile applications. <marks>1</marks>

- **Security vulnerabilities**:
Older systems often lack modern encryption standards and no longer receive security patches, making them more susceptible to cyberattacks. <marks>1</marks>

- **Lack of spare parts**:
If the aging hardware fails, finding replacement components for machines that are no longer in production can be nearly impossible. <marks>1</marks>

- **Performance bottlenecks**:
The hardware may not have the processing power or memory required to handle the volume of transactions expected in a modern banking environment. <marks>1</marks>

questions.specification:
A multinational retail chain is upgrading its inventory management software across its 500 global branches. 

The management is deciding whether to host the software locally at each branch or move to a **Software as a Service (SaaS)** model, and is considering different strategies for the transition.

parts.content: State **one** method of gathering information from stakeholders during the initial system investigation phase.
parts.markscheme:
- Interviews <marks>1</marks>
- Questionnaires / Surveys <marks>1</marks>
- Direct observation of current systems <marks>1</marks>
- Examination of existing documentation <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of [1]</note>

parts.content: A significant challenge in this project is moving records from the old software. Identify **three** problems that may arise during the data migration process.
parts.markscheme:
- Data loss due to transmission errors or system crashes <marks>1</marks>
- Incompatible file formats between the legacy system and the new software <marks>1</marks>
- Data validation errors (e.g., different conventions for dates or currencies) <marks>1</marks>
- Misinterpretation of data fields (different naming or logic) <marks>1</marks>
- Security risks or data breaches during the transfer <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [3]</note>

parts.content: The company is choosing between **parallel running** and **pilot running** for the implementation. Compare these two strategies.
parts.markscheme:
### Strategy #1 - Parallel Running
- The new system runs alongside the old one for a period, with results compared to ensure accuracy <marks>1</marks>
- Extremely safe/low risk because the old system is available as a backup if the new one fails <marks>1</marks>
- Very expensive and time-consuming as staff must enter all data into both systems <marks>1</marks>

### Strategy #2 - Pilot Running
- The new system is fully implemented in only one branch (the pilot site) before being rolled out to the rest <marks>1</marks>
- Risk is contained; if the system fails, only the pilot branch is affected rather than the whole company <marks>1</marks>
- Cheaper than parallel running as only one set of staff needs to operate the new system initially <marks>1</marks>
- Allows the company to refine the system based on feedback from the pilot site <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [5 max]</note>

parts.content: Identify **two** technical or financial benefits of utilizing a Software as a Service (SaaS) model for this global organization.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced initial capital expenditure as there is no need to purchase expensive server hardware <marks>1</marks>
- Easier scalability as the provider handles increases in data or user volume <marks>1</marks>
- Automatic updates and maintenance provided by the SaaS vendor <marks>1</marks>
- Lower IT maintenance costs for the organization as local troubleshooting is minimized <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [2 max]</note>

parts.content: Explain the importance of user documentation and discuss the advantages of providing it via an online help system rather than printed manuals.
parts.markscheme:
- User documentation is essential to help staff understand the system features and reduce the cost of technical support <marks>1</marks>
- Online systems are easier to update instantly when software changes occur <marks>1</marks>
- Online help can be searchable (e.g., using keywords), making it faster to find specific answers than a physical index <marks>1</marks>
- Online systems can include interactive elements or videos which improve the learning experience <marks>1</marks>
- Reduced environmental impact and lower distribution costs compared to printing and shipping manuals to 500 branches <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award up to [4 max]</note>

questions.specification: IB Computer Science Topic 1.1: Systems in Organizations

parts.content: A large retail chain is introducing a new inventory management system across all its stores. Explain **one** reason why the company might choose to use **parallel running** as its implementation strategy.
parts.markscheme:
Award [1] for identifying a valid reason and [1] for a corresponding explanation of its benefit in this context, up to **[2 max]**.

*   **Risk reduction / Safety net** <marks>1</marks>
    If the new inventory system fails or contains bugs, the old system is still operational, ensuring that business operations (like stock tracking) are not interrupted <marks>1</marks>.
*   **Verification of results / Data integrity** <marks>1</marks>
    Outputs from the new system can be compared against the old system to ensure that calculations and stock levels are being processed accurately before the old system is decommissioned <marks>1</marks>.
*   **Staff confidence / Training period** <marks>1</marks>
    Employees can get used to the new interface while still having the familiar system as a backup, reducing stress and errors during the transition <marks>1</marks>.

parts.content: Identify **one** potential issue that may occur during **data migration** when moving from a legacy system to a new system.
parts.markscheme:
Award **[1 max]** for any of the following:

*   **Data incompatibility** (e.g., different file formats or database structures) <marks>1</marks>
*   **Data loss** or corruption during the transfer process <marks>1</marks>
*   **Errors in the original data** (e.g., duplicate records or incomplete fields) being carried over <marks>1</marks>
*   **Difference in character encoding** (e.g., Unicode vs ASCII) <marks>1</marks>
*   **Validation mismatches** where the new system has stricter rules than the old one <marks>1</marks>

questions.specification:
A medical clinic is implementing a new digital Appointment Management System (AMS) to replace its current manual paper-based booking system. The AMS will handle patient scheduling, doctor availability, and automated SMS reminders.

To ensure the system meets the needs of all users, a design team has been hired to manage the development and implementation phases.

parts.content: Identify **two** stakeholders that should be consulted when designing this new system.
parts.markscheme:
Award **<marks>1</marks>** for each valid stakeholder identified, up to a maximum of **<marks>2</marks>**:

*   **Doctors/Medical Staff**: To ensure the scheduling interface meets their clinical availability and workflow.
*   **Administrative/Reception Staff**: As primary users who will manage the day-to-day booking and patient intake.
*   **Patients**: To gather requirements for user-facing features like SMS reminders or online portals.
*   **Clinic Managers/Owners**: To ensure the system aligns with business goals and reporting requirements.
*   **IT Support Staff**: To ensure the system is compatible with existing infrastructure and maintainable.

parts.content: Describe **one** disadvantage of using 'observation' as a method for gathering requirements from the clinic staff.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to **<marks>2</marks>**:

*   **Hawthorne Effect**: Staff members may change their natural behavior or work more efficiently/differently because they know they are being watched <marks>1</marks>, which leads to data that does not reflect typical daily operations. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Time-consuming**: It requires the observer to spend significant time watching various shifts <marks>1</marks>, which can be costly and delay the requirements gathering phase. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Outline **two** reasons why providing comprehensive user documentation is essential for the clinic's staff.
parts.markscheme:
Award **<marks>1</marks>** for each reason outlined, up to **<marks>2</marks>**:

*   **Training/Onboarding**: Documentation allows new staff members to learn the system independently, reducing the need for expensive formal training sessions. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Troubleshooting**: It provides a reference for existing staff to resolve minor technical issues or errors without needing to contact IT support. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Standardization**: Ensures that all staff use the system in a consistent manner, maintaining data integrity across the clinic. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: The development team conducts alpha testing on the new AMS. Outline **one** benefit of this type of testing.
parts.markscheme:
Award **<marks>1</marks>** for identifying a benefit and **<marks>1</marks>** for an elaboration, up to **<marks>2</marks>**:

*   **Early bug detection**: Developers can identify and fix critical logic errors or system crashes <marks>1</marks> before the software is ever seen by the end-users. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Controlled environment**: Testing is done by internal staff in a safe environment <marks>1</marks>, meaning there is no risk to actual patient data or clinical operations. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Outline **two** potential causes of data loss during the operation of the new system, other than hardware failure.
parts.markscheme:
Award **<marks>1</marks>** for each cause identified and briefly explained, up to **<marks>2</marks>**:

*   **Malicious activity**: Intentional actions such as viruses, malware, or ransomware that delete or encrypt patient databases. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Human error**: Accidental deletion of records or incorrect data entry by staff members who are not yet familiar with the interface. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Software bugs**: Unforeseen errors in the system's code that cause data to be corrupted or not saved correctly during high-traffic periods. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Natural disasters**: Events like fires or floods that destroy the physical location where the data is stored (if not properly backed up off-site). <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: State **one** reason for creating a prototype of the system's user interface.
parts.markscheme:
Award **<marks>1</marks>** for a valid reason:

*   To provide a visual model that allows stakeholders to give early feedback on the layout and usability before the final code is written. <marks>1</marks>

parts.content:
The clinic management is deciding between 'parallel running' and 'pilot running' for the system changeover. 

Compare these two implementation methods.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to **<marks>4</marks>**:

*   **Parallel running**: The new AMS and the old paper-based system are both operated fully at the same time for a period. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Pilot running**: The new AMS is implemented in only one small section or department of the clinic (e.g., the pharmacy or a single doctor's office) while the rest continues with the old system. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Comparison (Risk)**: Parallel running is much lower risk because the old system is still current and acts as a complete backup; Pilot running only protects the organization outside of the pilot group—if the system fails, the pilot group is disrupted. <marks>1</marks>
*   **Comparison (Cost/Efficiency)**: Parallel running is much more expensive and labor-intensive as staff must perform double data entry; Pilot running is more cost-effective as it only involves a small group and allows issues to be fixed before a full rollout. <marks>1</marks>

<note>
- For full marks, both methods must be described and compared (e.g., regarding risk, cost, or workload).
</note>

parts.content: A retail business chooses to implement a new point-of-sale system via **direct changeover**. Outline **one** risk associated with this method of implementation.
parts.markscheme:
- Absence of a fallback system <marks>1</marks>
  - If the new software encounters a critical failure, there is no legacy system running to revert to, which can cause significant operational downtime <marks>1</marks>

- Data migration errors <marks>1</marks>
  - If data is not transferred correctly from the old system, it may be lost or corrupted without an active original system to act as a reference or backup <marks>1</marks>

- User stress and errors <marks>1</marks>
  - Staff must be fully proficient with the new interface immediately, which increases the likelihood of human error during the high-pressure transition period <marks>1</marks>

*Note: Award maximum 2 marks for any ONE risk with explanation.*

[2 marks]

questions.specification: A global healthcare provider is replacing its decentralized paper-based records with a unified cloud-based management system. The project team is currently finalizing the transition plan to ensure all medical staff are prepared for the change and that historical patient records are accurately moved to the new platform.

parts.content: Describe **two** methods that could be used to gather requirements for the new system from the medical staff.
parts.markscheme:
**Interviews**: 
Conducting one-on-one sessions with doctors or nurses to gather detailed qualitative feedback and understand specific workflows. <marks>2</marks>

**Questionnaires/Surveys**: 
Distributing digital or paper forms to a large number of staff to gather statistical data and identify common needs across departments. <marks>2</marks>

**Observations**: 
Watching staff perform their daily tasks in the current system to identify bottlenecks or requirements they might not mention in an interview. <marks>2</marks>

**Examining current documentation**: 
Reviewing existing paper forms and record-keeping procedures to understand the data structures currently in use. <marks>2</marks>

Note: Award **[4 max]**. Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying a method and <marks>1</marks> for a description/advantage of that method.

parts.content: State **two** reasons why providing comprehensive user documentation is essential for this new healthcare system.
parts.markscheme:
- To reduce the cost of technical support/help desk calls by allowing users to solve their own problems; <marks>1</marks>
- To improve the efficiency of the medical staff by providing clear instructions on complex features; <marks>1</marks>
- To act as a reference for staff who only use certain features of the system infrequently; <marks>1</marks>
- To reduce the learning curve for new employees joining the hospital group; <marks>1</marks>

Note: Award **[2 max]**.

parts.content: The healthcare provider chooses to implement the new system using **pilot running**. Explain why this strategy is appropriate for this organization.
parts.markscheme:
- The system is first installed in only one department or one small clinic (the pilot site); <marks>1</marks>
- This limits the risk to the rest of the organization if the system fails or has major bugs; <marks>1</marks>
- Once the system is proven to be successful at the pilot site, it can be rolled out to the rest of the hospital group; <marks>1</marks>
- Feedback from the pilot site can be used to make improvements before the full-scale implementation; <marks>1</marks>
- It is more cost-effective than parallel running as it does not require all staff to run two systems at once; <marks>1</marks>

Note: Award **[3 max]**.

parts.content: Outline **two** training methods that could be used to prepare medical staff for the new system.
parts.markscheme:
- **Formal classroom training**: An instructor leads a group of staff through the system in a controlled environment, allowing for immediate questions and structured learning. <marks>2</marks>

- **Self-instruction/Online tutorials**: Staff use manuals or web-based videos to learn at their own pace, which is flexible and reduces the need for expensive external trainers. <marks>2</marks>

- **Remote/Online training (e.g., via webinar)**: Allows staff across different geographical locations to be trained simultaneously by a central expert without travel costs. <marks>2</marks>

Note: Award **[4 max]**. Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying a method and <marks>1</marks> for an outline of its characteristics or advantages.

parts.content: Outline **one** potential problem that could occur during the data migration from paper records to the cloud-based system.
parts.markscheme:
- **Data Incompatibility**: The data format used in the old paper records (e.g., handwritten notes) may be difficult to digitize or may not fit the standardized fields in the new database; <marks>1</marks>
- **Incomplete Data Transfer**: Some historical records may be lost or misinterpreted during the manual entry/scanning process, leading to missing patient information; <marks>1</marks>
- **Data Currency Issues**: Changes made to patient records during the transition period may not be reflected in the new system, resulting in outdated information being migrated; <marks>1</marks>

Note: Award **[2 max]**. Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying a problem and <marks>1</marks> for an expansion of why this is a problem in the context of the migration.

parts.content: Identify **one** area of feasibility that should be evaluated during the planning phase of a new system.
parts.markscheme:
- technical feasibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- economic feasibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- legal feasibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- operational feasibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
- schedule feasibility <marks>1 mark</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: Describe **one** way an organization can evaluate the **operational feasibility** of a proposed system.
parts.markscheme:
- distribute surveys or questionnaires to the current employees <marks>1 mark</marks>
- to determine whether they possess the required skills or are willing to adapt to the new system <marks>1 mark</marks>

OR

- perform an observation of existing workplace workflows <marks>1 mark</marks>
- to assess whether the proposed system will effectively integrate with current business practices <marks>1 mark</marks>

OR

- conduct interviews with department heads and potential users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- to identify any possible organizational resistance to change or specific training requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Outline **one** disadvantage of using a phased conversion strategy when implementing a new system.
parts.markscheme:
– A phased conversion takes significantly longer to complete compared to other methods because the system is introduced in stages. <marks>1</marks>
– This results in a period where employees must manage data across two different systems, which can lead to confusion, increased workload, or data inconsistencies. <marks>1</marks>
– Training costs may increase as staff need to learn how the old and new systems interact during the transition period. <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying a disadvantage and <marks>1</marks> for an expansion up to <marks>2 max</marks>.</note>

parts.content: Outline **one** advantage of using questionnaires to gather user requirements during the planning phase of a new system.
parts.markscheme:
– Questionnaires allow for the collection of data from a large number of stakeholders simultaneously. <marks>1</marks>
– This provides a cost-effective and time-efficient way to gather quantitative data compared to conducting individual interviews. <marks>1</marks>
– Responses can be kept anonymous, which may encourage stakeholders to provide more honest feedback about the current system's limitations. <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying an advantage and <marks>1</marks> for an expansion up to <marks>2 max</marks>.</note>

parts.content: A multinational company has recently updated its office productivity suite. Suggest **two** reasons why the company might choose to provide **online training** for its employees rather than **face-to-face classroom training**.
parts.markscheme:
- It is more cost-effective as it eliminates travel and accommodation expenses for staff and trainers <marks>A1</marks>
- It allows for greater flexibility because employees can complete the training at a time that fits their work schedule <marks>A1</marks>
- It provides a consistent training experience as all employees across different regions access the same standardized content <marks>A1</marks>
- Recorded sessions allow staff to revisit specific modules if they encounter difficulties during actual use <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>
Award **[2 max]**.
Accept any valid advantage of online/remote training over physical classroom settings.
</note>
